title: VisNec: Measuring and Leveraging Visual Necessity for Multimodal Instruction Tuning
abstract: arXiv:2603.01195v2 Announce Type: replace-cross  Abstract: The effectiveness of multimodal instruction tuning depends not only on dataset scale, but critically on whether training samples genuinely require visual reasoning. However, existing instruction datasets often contain a substantial portion of visually redundant samples (solvable from text alone), as well as multimodally misaligned supervision that can degrade learning. To address this, we propose VisNec (Visual Necessity Score), a principled data selection framework that measures the marginal contribution of visual input during instruction tuning. By comparing predictive loss with and without visual context, VisNec identifies whether a training instance is vision-critical, redundant, or misaligned. To preserve task diversity, we combine VisNec with semantic clustering and select high-necessity samples within each cluster. Across 10 downstream benchmarks, training on only 15% of the LLaVA-665K dataset selected by VisNec achieves

Title: VisNec: Measuring and Leveraging Visual Necessity for Multimodal Instruction Tuning
Abstract: arXiv:2603.01195v2 Announce Type: replace-cross  Abstract: The effectiveness of multimodal instruction tuning depends not only on dataset scale, but critically on whether training samples genuinely require visual reasoning. However, existing instruction datasets often contain a substantial portion of visually redundant samples (solvable from text alone), as well as multimodally misaligned supervision that can degrade learning. To address this, we propose VisNec (Visual Necessity Score), a principled data selection framework that measures the marginal contribution of visual input during instruction tuning. By comparing predictive loss with and without visual context, VisNec identifies whether a training instance is vision-critical, redundant, or misaligned. To preserve task diversity, we combine VisNec with semantic clustering and select high-necessity samples within each cluster. Across 10 downstream benchmarks, training on only 15% of the LLaVA-665K dataset selected by VisNec achieves
translated_title: VisNec：测量和利用视觉必要性进行多模态指令微调
translated_abstract: arXiv:2603.01195v2 公告类型：替换交叉 摘要：多模态指令微调的有效性不仅取决于数据集规模，更关键的是训练样本是否真正需要视觉推理。然而，现有的指令数据集通常包含大量视觉冗余样本（仅从文本即可解决），以及可能降低学习效果的多模态对齐不当的监督信号。为解决这一问题，我们提出了VisNec（视觉必要性评分），一种基于原则的数据选择框架，用于衡量指令微调过程中视觉输入的边际贡献。通过比较有视觉上下文和无视觉上下文时的预测损失，VisNec能够识别训练实例是视觉关键的、冗余的还是对齐不当的。为了保持任务多样性，我们将VisNec与语义聚类相结合，在每个聚类中选择高必要性的样本。在10个下游基准测试中，仅使用VisNec从LLaVA-665K数据集中选择的15%数据进行训练，便达到了……
tldr: 提出VisNec框架，通过测量视觉输入的边际贡献来筛选多模态指令微调中真正需要视觉推理的样本，仅用15%数据即可在多个基准上取得优异效果。
en_tdlr: VisNec proposes a framework that measures the marginal contribution of visual input to select genuinely vision-required samples for multimodal instruction tuning, achieving strong performance across benchmarks with only 15% of data.
